old_claim.note: Early Electromagnetism is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim.note: Faraday's 1831 disk established mechanical-to-electrical induction; the 1866 self-excited dynamo was the later practical machine stage using field-current feedback.

source_shape.source_locator: Pages 8 and 27: Faraday's 1831 disk demonstrated electrical generation but remained an experimental result; the review then identifies the 1866 self-excited dynamo as the later machine development.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The Smithsonian history separates Faraday's first rotating disk generator from the later self-excited machines and presents them as successive generator stages.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Pointing to the existing Faraday disk node preserves the concrete generator lineage while avoiding a second node that launders the same 1831 milestone under a broader dynamo label.

ontology_before: Dynamo duplicated the 1831 Faraday origin and pointed only to a broad electromagnetism category as its historical predecessor.
ontology_after: Self-Excited Industrial Dynamo is a distinct 1866 stage that directly follows the separate Faraday Disk Generator node.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: electric_generators_dynamos is the historical predecessor and electromagnetism_early_discoveries is no longer a redundant direct edge.
would_reject_if: The dynamo node is reverted to Faraday's 1831 disk rather than the 1866 self-excited machine.
A stronger source demonstrates no historical or technical lineage from Faraday's generator to self-excited dynamos.
The separate electric_generators_dynamos node is removed and its 1831 scope is intentionally merged.
short_reason: The precise predecessor is Faraday's generator, not a duplicate 1831 origin.
